perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed and interpreted, and its performance shall be governed by substantive laws of the state of Connecticut, U.S.A., without recourse to its conflict of laws, rules or principles.
perturbation.changed_text: During the term of this Agreement, the receiving party agrees to hold in confidence and not disclose to any person or persons, other than its employees with a need to know, or use except for the purpose of this agreement, for a period of fifteen (15) years from the date of each disclosure, any and all Proprietary Information disclosed in writing and identified as Proprietary Information by the disclosing party. If Proprietary Information is disclosed orally or in other than written form, it must be identified as Proprietary Information at the time of disclosure and summarized in writing and identified as Proprietary Information by the disclosing party within thirty (30) days from the date of the disclosure. It is understood that the foregoing obligation of confidentiality does not apply to: a) information which at the time of disclosure is in the public domain; b) information which is published or otherwise becomes part of the public domain through no fault of the receiving party after the disclosure hereunder; c) information which the receiving party can demonstrate by reasonably convincing evidence is already known or in the possession of the receiving party at the time of disclosure hereunder; d) information that the receiving party can show was received by it after the time of the disclosure hereunder from a third party on a non-confidential basis who did not acquire such information directly or indirectly from the disclosing party under an obligation of confidence; or <PAGE> 26 -26- e) information that is developed by an employee of the receiving party independent of any such disclosure under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The choice of law clause is moved into the Confidentiality section, obscuring its relevance for governing the entire agreement, potentially making it difficult to enforce specific clauses under Connecticut law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Connecticut Unfair Trade Practices Act (CUTPA), C.G.S. § 42-110b, gives consumers a cause of action for unfair or deceptive trade practices. By burying the choice of law clause within a confidentiality clause, it could be argued that it obscures the consumer's right to bring a CUTPA claim in Connecticut if they are located in Connecticut and were harmed by a deceptive practice arising from the contract.

Previous Next (a) No person shall engage in unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing subsection (a) of this section, the commissioner and the courts of this state shall be guided by interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the federal courts to Section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 USC 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The commissioner may, in accordance with chapter 54, establish by regulation acts, practices or methods which shall be deemed to be unfair or deceptive in violation of subsection (a) of this section. Such regulations shall not be inconsistent with the rules, regulations and decisions of the federal trade commission and the federal courts in interpreting the provisions of the Federal Trade Commission Act. (d) It is the intention of the legislature that this chapter be remedial and be so construed. (P.A. 73-615, S. 2, 16; P.A. 75-618, S. 1, 11; P.A. 76-303, S. 1, 4.) History; P.A. 75-618 rephrased provisions for clarity and consistency; P.A. 76-303 deleted provision in Subsec. (a) which established unfa
perturbation.justification: The relocation of the choice of law clause into the confidentiality section creates a structural flaw that could obscure a consumer's rights under the Connecticut Unfair Trade Practices Act (CUTPA), C.G.S. § 42-110b. According to the law explanation, burying the choice of law clause within a confidentiality clause makes it difficult for a consumer to bring a CUTPA claim in Connecticut. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that 'No person shall engage in unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce' based on Connecticut General Statutes § 42-110b. By obscuring the choice of law, it makes it more challenging for a consumer to seek protection under Connecticut law, creating a structural flaw as mentioned in the definition.

perturbation.original_text: Either party upon thirty (30) days notice given in writing to the other party may terminate this Agreement. However, termination of the Agreement will not affect the confidentiality and non-use obligations of either party.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party upon thirty (30) days notice given in writing to the other party may terminate this Agreement. However, termination of the Agreement will not affect the confidentiality and non-use obligations of either party. AGREED: BALLARD POWER SYSTEMS INC. UCAR CARBON COMPANY INC.
perturbation.explanation: The termination clause is made to include part of the signatures, and the signatures are removed from their dedicated section, reducing its clarity. It may imply agreement on termination terms only, and not the entire agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law principles regarding offer and acceptance
perturbation.law_explanation: Fundamental contract law principles require clear offer and acceptance. Moving the signatures into the termination clause suggests that only the termination clause was explicitly agreed upon, potentially creating ambiguity and weakening the enforceability of the entire contract. The Uniform Commercial Code, while more relevant to sales of goods, emphasizes the importance of clear manifestation of intent to be bound, and this obfuscation works against that principle.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: offer | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you offer Offer is part of contract negotiations where a party agrees to do or not do something in exchange for consideration . An offer must be stated and delivered in a way that would lead a reasonable person to expect a binding contract to arise from its acceptance . An offer gives the offeree the ability to accept and form an enforceable contract. When a valid offer is made, an acceptance by the offeree creates a binding contract. An offeror generally cannot retract the offer once the other party has accepted. A court may not find a binding contract regardless, though, if the negotiation did not involve consideration, involves an illusory promise , or if the offeror did not have the capacity to enter into the contract. However, a contract offer can be withdrawn by notification to the offeree of the intent to withdraw the offer before the offeree has accepted. Offerors typically can withdraw an offer before acceptance in any contract negotiation unless the parties have created an enforceable option contract . If the offeree rejects the offer, the offer can no longer be accepted by the offeree. An offer can also be made in the form of a counteroffer . The offeree that receives the initial offer can make a new offer of their own instead of accepting the initial offer. This counteroffer acts as a rejection of the initial offer and creates an entirely new offer that can be accepted or rejected. [Last reviewed in August of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 2-206. Offer and Acceptance in Formation of Contract. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-206. Offer and Acceptance in Formation of Contract. (1) Unless otherwise unambiguously indicated by the language or circumstances (a) an offer to make a contract shall be construed as inviting acceptance in any manner and by any medium reasonable in the circumstances; (b) an order or other offer to buy goods for prompt or current shipment shall be construed as inviting acceptance either by a prompt promise to ship or by the prompt or current shipment of conforming or non-conforming goods, but such a shipment of non-conforming goods does not constitute an acceptance if the seller seasonably notifies the buyer that the shipment is offered only as an accommodation to the buyer. (2) Where the beginning of a requested performance is a reasonable mode of acceptance an offeror who is not notified of acceptance within a reasonable time may treat the offer as having lapsed before acceptance. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a structural flaw by embedding the signatures within the termination clause, potentially undermining the clear manifestation of intent to be bound by the entire contract, as the Law Explanation describes. Scraped Law Snippet 1 highlights that an offer must be stated in a way that would lead a reasonable person to expect a binding contract to arise from its acceptance. By moving the signatures into a specific clause, it creates ambiguity as to whether the parties intended to be bound by the entire contract or only the termination clause. This ambiguity could lead to disputes regarding the enforceability of other clauses within the agreement, supporting the 'YES' determination.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party agrees not to copy any Proprietary Information without the written permission of the disclosing party, and shall return such Proprietary Information and any copies when requested to do so by the disclosing party during the term of this Agreement
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, each party is willing to disclose to the other <PAGE> 25 -25- party its Proprietary Information for the specific purposes of this Agreement under the following conditions: 1. During the term of this Agreement, the receiving party agrees to hold in confidence and not disclose to any person or persons, other than its employees with a need to know, or use except for the purpose of this agreement, for a period of fifteen (15) years from the date of each disclosure, any and all Proprietary Information disclosed in writing and identified as Proprietary Information by the disclosing party. If Proprietary Information is disclosed orally or in other than written form, it must be identified as Proprietary Information at the time of disclosure and summarized in writing and identified as Proprietary Information by the disclosing party within thirty (30) days from the date of the disclosure. It is understood that the foregoing obligation of confidentiality does not apply to: a) information which at the time of disclosure is in the public domain; b) information which is published or otherwise becomes part of the public domain through no fault of the receiving party after the disclosure hereunder; c) information which the receiving party can demonstrate by reasonably convincing evidence is already known or in the possession of the receiving party at the time of disclosure hereunder; d) information that the receiving party can show was received by it after the time of the disclosure hereunder from a third party on a non-confidential basis who did not acquire such information directly or indirectly from the disclosing party under an obligation of confidence; or <PAGE> 26 -26- e) information that is developed by an employee of the receiving party independent of any such disclosure under this Agreement. 2. The receiving party agrees to use at least the same degree of care in maintaining the other party's Proprietary Information confidential as it does for maintaining the confidentiality of its own Proprietary Information of a similar nature.
perturbation.explanation: The clause prohibiting copying of proprietary information is moved into the confidentiality section, and the text where it was, is replaced with the clause above. This change reduces the visibility of the restrictions on copying, potentially undermining protection against unauthorized duplication.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act defines misappropriation to include unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. While a confidentiality agreement itself is a measure to protect trade secrets, obscuring the specific prohibition against copying weakens the overall protection. A court may be less likely to find misappropriation if the agreement doesn't explicitly highlight the importance of not copying the information.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text contained a clear prohibition against copying proprietary information. The changed text moves this prohibition into the confidentiality section, potentially obscuring it. The Law Explanation suggests that obscuring the specific prohibition against copying weakens the overall protection of trade secrets and could make it harder to prove misappropriation. The Perturbation Explanation also notes the reduced visibility of the restrictions on copying. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines a trade secret as 'a secret, commercially valuable plan, formula, process, or device'. While this snippet does not explicitly address copying, the fact that the definition emphasizes the 'secret' nature of the information supports the idea that measures to protect that secrecy, including clear prohibitions against copying, are important. The relocation and potential downplaying of the copying prohibition creates a structural flaw by making it less clear and accessible, thus contradicting the need to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS, UCAR CARBON possesses certain proprietary information (hereinafter UCAR CARBON Proprietary Information) relating to the manufacture of flexible graphite for use in fuel cells: WHEREAS, BALLARD possesses certain proprietary information (hereinafter BALLARD Proprietary Information) relating to fuel cells and components for fuel cells. WHEREAS, UCAR CARBON and BALLARD desire to exchange such proprietary information (hereinafter individually and collectively referred to as "Proprietary Information") for the purpose of UCAR CARBON supplying flexible graphite produced employing UCAR CARBON Proprietary Information to BALLARD under a separate purchase order.
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, UCAR CARBON possesses certain proprietary information (hereinafter UCAR CARBON Proprietary Information) relating to the manufacture of flexible graphite for use in fuel cells: WHEREAS, BALLARD possesses certain proprietary information (hereinafter BALLARD Proprietary Information) relating to fuel cells and components for fuel cells. WHEREAS, UCAR CARBON and BALLARD desire to exchange such proprietary information (hereinafter individually and collectively referred to as "Proprietary Information") for the purpose of UCAR CARBON supplying flexible graphite produced employing UCAR CARBON Proprietary Information to BALLARD under a separate purchase order. 5. This Agreement shall be construed and interpreted, and its performance shall be governed by substantive laws of the state of Connecticut, U.S.A., without recourse to its conflict of laws, rules or principles.
perturbation.explanation: The choice of law clause is moved into the recitals section, obscuring its relevance for governing the entire agreement, potentially making it difficult to enforce specific clauses under Connecticut law. This renders the location less obvious to the parties to the contract and any third-party observers.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Connecticut Unfair Trade Practices Act (CUTPA), C.G.S. § 42-110b, gives consumers a cause of action for unfair or deceptive trade practices. By moving the choice of law clause within the 'Whereas' statements, it's less prominent, potentially weakening a consumer's right to bring a CUTPA claim in Connecticut if they were harmed by a deceptive practice arising from the contract. Recitals are often considered introductory and not necessarily binding, further reducing the enforceability of the clause in that section.

perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation and law explanation both indicate that moving the choice of law clause into the recitals weakens its enforceability, especially concerning consumer protection under CUTPA. The law snippet from Connecticut General Statutes § 42-110b, regarding unfair trade practices, supports the idea that consumers have rights under CUTPA. By obscuring the choice of law, the contract makes it more difficult to invoke Connecticut law to protect consumer rights. This constitutes a structural flaw that contradicts the remedial intent of CUTPA, making it harder for consumers to bring a claim in Connecticut.
